name: Linear Programming
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 12.1 LPP — Setup & Terminology
sections.body:
A linear programming problem (LPP) seeks to optimise (maximise or minimise) a linear objective function $Z=ax+by$ subject to linear constraints in the variables $x,y\geq 0$.

Standard form.

Maximise (or Minimise) $Z=ax+by$, subject to $a_i x+b_i y\leq(\geq)c_i,\;i=1,\dots,m$ and $x,y\geq 0$ (non-negativity).

Key terms: decision variables ($x,y$), constraints (linear inequalities), non-negativity restrictions, feasible region (set of all $(x,y)$ satisfying all constraints), feasible solution, optimal solution (maximises or minimises $Z$).

sections.heading: 12.2 Graphical Method
sections.body:
For two-variable LPPs we use the corner-point method:

  1. Write all constraints. Treat each $\leq$ or $\geq$ as an equation, plot the line, and shade the half-plane that satisfies the inequality.
  2. The intersection of all shaded regions is the feasible region.
  3. Identify the corner points (vertices) of this region.
  4. Compute $Z=ax+by$ at every corner point.
  5. The maximum (or minimum) of these values is the optimal value, and the corresponding vertex is the optimal solution.
keyFormulae: Optimum at vertex of feasible region

sections.heading: 12.3 Bounded vs Unbounded Feasible Regions
sections.body:
If the feasible region is bounded, the objective function attains BOTH a maximum and a minimum at corner points.

If the feasible region is unbounded, $Z$ may not attain a max/min. Test: after finding the candidate optimum $M$ at a corner point, check whether the open half-plane $ax+by>M$ (for max) or $ax+by

  • If yes — there is no maximum (or minimum).
  • If no — $M$ is the maximum (or minimum).

sections.heading: 12.4 Special Cases & Multiple Solutions
sections.body:
Multiple optimal solutions. If the line $ax+by=Z_{\max}$ is parallel to a boundary edge of the feasible region, then every point on that edge gives the same optimal $Z$. Solutions are then infinitely many along the edge.

Infeasibility. If constraints contradict each other, the feasible region is empty and the LPP has no solution.

Redundant constraint. A constraint that does not change the feasible region.
keyFormulae: Edge parallel to $Z=k$: infinitely many optima

sections.heading: 12.5 Common Application Types
sections.body:
Standard problem types:

  • Manufacturing / production: limited resources (labour, machine time, raw material) split between two products to maximise profit.
  • Diet problem: choose quantities of foods to meet nutritional minimums at minimum cost.
  • Transportation: ship goods from sources to destinations at minimum cost respecting supply and demand.
  • Allocation / blending: mix products to meet specs at minimum cost.

Modelling steps.

  1. Identify decision variables.
  2. Write the objective function.
  3. Express each restriction as a linear inequality.
  4. Add non-negativity $x,y\geq 0$.
  5. Solve graphically.

summary: An LPP optimises a linear objective $Z=ax+by$ over a region defined by linear inequalities and $x,y\geq 0$. Solve graphically: plot constraints, identify the feasible region, evaluate $Z$ at each corner point, pick the optimal value. Bounded feasible regions guarantee optima at vertices; unbounded regions need an extra check via the open half-plane test. Multiple optima occur when the objective line is parallel to a boundary edge.
pitfalls: Shading the wrong side of an inequality — substitute a test point like $(0,0)$ to verify.
Skipping the unbounded-region check and reporting a false maximum.
Computing $Z$ only at one or two vertices rather than ALL corner points.
Forgetting that integer constraints (when applicable) may rule out fractional vertex solutions.
